segments.s031-g1: چو آمد begins a temporal subordinate clause: چو = 'when' and آمد is the past tense 'he came'. به نزدیک اروندرود is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the Arvand River' (به + نزدیک + proper name). Word order places the time/place phrase before the main clause.
segments.s031-g2: فرستاد is the simple past transitive 'he sent'. زی رودبانان درود is the indirect object + direct object: زی (an archaic/prepositional element here meaning roughly 'to/onto') marks the recipients (رودبانان, 'boatmen') and درود is the thing sent, 'greeting(s)'.
segments.s031-g3: بران is a deictic/transition word 'on that / thereupon' linking to the previous action. رودبان is the speaker 'the boatman' and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'; the following words address the king (پیروز شاه) — an address formed after the verb, typical in older poetic word order.
segments.s031-g4: که introduces reported content 'that / (saying) that'. کشتی is 'the ship'. برافگن is a poetic/archaic participial idea meaning here 'having been launched/raised (sails)' (a causative/participial nuance). هم‌اکنون is the adverb 'right now' and به راه = 'on the way' (be + rāh). The whole clause reports the boatman's information about the ship's departure.
segments.s031-g5: مرا (object 'me') appears before the instrument phrase با سپاهم ('with my army'). رسان is the imperative/causative 'bring/transport (me)'; بدان سو ('to that side') is the destination (ب + دان + سو form an older compound meaning 'to that side'). Note the poem's orthography splits elements that function together (مرا, بدان).
segments.s031-g6: از اینها ('of/from these [men]') sets the group; کسی را is 'anyone' as definite object (را marking the object). بدین سو is 'to this side' (ب + دین = به + این, an older fused prepositional form) and ممان is an imperative/negative-root verb meaning 'do not withhold/prevent' — so the clause commands that none of these be stopped from going to that side.
segments.s031-g7: بدان is the prepositional compound 'to that' (به + آن); تا introduces a purpose/subjunctive clause 'so that'. گذر یابم is a subjunctive construction: گذر 'passage/crossing' + یابم (subjunctive/potential of یافتن) 'may I find' = 'so that I may find a crossing'. از روی آب specifies the locus 'over/on the water' (از + روی + آب).
segments.s031-g8: به کشتی و زورق هم is an instrumental phrase 'by ship and by small-boat as well' (به here used as a means/agent marker). اندر شتاب is an older adverbial phrase 'in haste' (اندر = 'in/within', شتاب = 'haste'). Together they qualify how the crossing is to be effected.
segments.s031-g25: This is a past‑tense, plural clause: به + خشکی + رسیدند = 'they reached/arrived at the dry land.' The preposition به here marks motion toward/into (classical Persian usage). سر کینه‌جوی is a compact noun phrase: سر 'chief/leader' modifying کینه‌جوی ('one who seeks grudges' or 'the vengeful'), forming the group/agent that performs the verb رسیدند; classical poetry often omits the ezāfe.
segments.s031-g26: نهادند (past, 3rd‑pl) + روی = 'they set (their) face'; نهادن here means 'to set/put' and, with روی ('face'), forms the idiom 'روی نهادن به' = 'to turn one's face toward / to direct oneself toward.' به + بیت‌المقدس marks the destination ('toward Jerusalem'). Word order (preposition + place + verb + object) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s031-g27: که introduces a explanatory/relative clause ('for/that' / 'who'), followed by بر + پهلوانی + زبان راندند. بر here means 'against,' and زبان راندن is an idiom meaning 'to use the tongue' in the sense 'to taunt/utter abusive speech.' راندند is past, 3rd‑pl. Note the noun پهلوانی is used substantively to refer to the champion/heroic figure (classical usage permits such nominal forms).
segments.s031-g28: همی is a discourse particle (here an emphatic/continuous marker). The string کنگ دژهودجش is an obscure or corrupted name/title treated as the object of خواندند; خواندند (past, 3rd‑pl) here uses its sense 'to call/namesake' ('they called him ...' / 'they named him ...'). Both lexical items are irregular/obscure in the manuscript and are presented as fixed names.
segments.s031-g29: به + تازی here means 'in (the) Arabic (language / among Arabs)' — the preposition به marks the language/register. کنون is an adverb 'now.' خانه پاک is the noun phrase 'the Pure House.' دان (past, 3rd‑pl) means 'they considered / deemed' so the whole clause means 'they now, in Arabic, called/considered it the Pure House.'
segments.s031-g30: برآورده functions here as an adjectival/past‑participle notion 'upraised/erected' modifying ایوان ('iwan, lofty porch'); ضحاک is the possessor/name attached to ایوان (Zahhāk's iwan). دان again is the verb 'they considered/regarded,' so the phrase means 'and they regarded it as Zahhāk's upraised iwan.' Note classical ordering places modifiers before the noun.
segments.s031-g31: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. از دشت نزدیک شهر آمدند is literally 'they came from the plain near the city'; از marks origin, نزدیک modifies شهر ('near the city'), and آمدند is past, 3rd‑pl 'they came/approached.' This is a standard temporal + motion clause in narrative.
segments.s031-g32: کزان = 'from which / from that (city)' introduces origin for the following noun. شهر جوینده = 'a seeker from the city' (جوینده = seeker). بهر آمدند uses بهر ('toward / to') plus آمدند (past, 3rd‑pl) to mean 'came forth toward / came to (them).' The verb is plural and agrees with the agent 'the seeker(s)' who issue from the city.
segments.s031-g9: نیاورد is a past negative verb (he/it 'did not bring') placed before its noun phrases, a common poetic inversion. Here کشتی is the thing 'boat/ship' (the object), and نگهبان رود functions as the agent/subject 'the (river) guardian' (the ezāfe is omitted in the compact poetic phrase), so the clause means 'the river-guardian did not bring a boat.'
segments.s031-g10: نیامد is the past negative 'did not come' and refers back to the (same) guardian; بگفت is the narrative past 'he said' (classical prefix بـ in reported speech). The sequence فریدون فرود is reported speech condensed in epic style: Fereydun is named and فرود ('descent/alighting') is given as the utterance or command — i.e. ‘Fereydun cried, “Alight!”’.
segments.s031-g11: چنین داد پاسخ = 'gave such an answer'; چنین (thus) modifies داد (gave). پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'. The conjunction که introduces a reported clause ('that ...'), and شاه جهان is the subject of the following reported clause ('the king of the world').
segments.s031-g12: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین again marking manner). با من سخن is a light noun phrase meaning 'spoke with me' ('سخن' = speech, preceded by the preposition با + pronoun من). در نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret/in private'. Note the somewhat archaic word order (چنین گفت با من سخن در نهان) where English would typically be 'he spoke to me thus in private.'
segments.s031-g13: که introduces indirect speech/exhortation 'that / saying'. مگذار is a negative imperative 'do not allow/let'. یک پشه را is the definite object marked by را ('even a single gnat/insignificant creature'); تا ... نخست sets a temporal limit 'until ... first', so this clause means 'do not let even a single gnat until first ...'.
segments.s031-g14: جوازی بیابی is a purpose/temporal complement to تا: جواز (permission) with بیابی (subjunctive/present-form of یابیدن, 'you obtain/you secure'). The coordination و links the next requirement مهری درست — literally 'a correct/proper mehr'. In context مهری refers to the marriage portion (mahr) and درست means 'proper/arranged properly'; grammatically بیابی is a subjunctive/optative dependent on تا (’until you have obtained’).
segments.s031-g15: فریدون چو بشنید شد خشمناک is a temporal clause: چو ('when') + بشنید ('he heard' — past) + شد خشمناک ('he became angry' — شد as the copula/auxiliary plus adjective). Word order follows classical/poetic patterns but the meaning is straightforward: 'When Fereydun heard, he became angry.'
segments.s031-g16: ازان ژرف دریا نیامدش باک literally 'from that deep sea fear did not come to him.' ازان = 'from that'; ژرف دریا = 'deep sea' (adjective before noun in poetic order); نیامدش = نیامد + enclitic ش (to him) 'did not come to him'; باک = 'fear/dread' — the construction means 'he felt no fear of that deep sea.'
segments.s031-g41: به یارانش گفت is a simple past clause: به (to) + یارانش (his companions; یاران 'companions' + enclitic ـش 'his') + گفت (he said). آنکه begins a relative clause ('the one who / whoever'). بر تیره خاک is a small prepositional phrase: بر (on/upon) + تیره (adjective 'dark') + خاک (earth/soil); word order is poetic but means 'upon/onto dark earth'.
segments.s031-g42: برآرد is a third-person singular form meaning 'brings forth/raises' (classical usage can have a potential/relative sense: 'whoever brings up...'). چنین ('thus/so') modifies the result. برز جای is 'a high/exalted place' (برز 'high/exalted' + جای 'place'). از مغاک is a prepositional phrase 'out of the abyss' (از + مغاک). The clause completes the relative started by آنکه: 'whoever raises (a heap of) dark earth will thus raise a lofty place out of the abyss.'
segments.s031-g43: بترسم is first-person present 'I fear' (classical form). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/just' or 'still' in older Persian) adding emphasis to the verb. زانکه is the subordinating conjunction 'because'. با او جهان literally 'with him the world' is a poetic word order (normally جهان با او), meaning 'the world with him' or 'the world shares/keeps (something) with him' — با (with) + او (him) + جهان (the world).
segments.s031-g44: مگر here functions as a modal particle meaning something like 'perhaps' (classical/poetic usage), introducing a possibility. راز دارد is 'has a secret' (راز 'secret' + دارد 'has'). یکی در نهان is an appositive/clarifying phrase: یکی ('a certain one' / 'one') + در نهان ('in hiddenness' = 'hidden inside'); together: 'a secret hidden within.'
segments.s031-g45: بباید که is an impersonal/modal construction 'it is necessary that / we must'. ما را here is a dative/benefactive construction ('for us' / 'we' as the ones affected) using the object marker را in older Persian. بدین is the fused prepositional phrase به + این = 'to/into this'; note the wordlist splits it, but here it means 'into this'. جای تنگ is 'narrow place' (جای 'place' + تنگ 'narrow'). So the clause says essentially 'it is necessary that we (go) into this narrow place' with the following clause specifying urgency.
segments.s031-g46: شتابیدن is the infinitive 'to hurry'. آید here is a third-person singular verb used idiomatically with a dative subject (ما را ...) + infinitive to express that something will befall/come upon us or that we will have to do it ('we must / will have to hurry'). به‌روز درنگ is a nominal phrase 'a day's delay' (به‌روز 'of the day' = 'day's' + درنگ 'delay'); in this construction it indicates what would occur or be at stake (i.e. 'a day's delay would happen/befall us if we do not hurry').
segments.s031-g47: بگفت is past 'he said/spoke'. و is the coordinating 'and'. به گرز گران دست برد is an idiomatic phrase: به + گرز گران ('at/to the heavy mace') + دست برد (took [hold of] / laid hand on). دست برد literally 'put hand' and here means 'took up / seized' (classical phrasing: دست بردن به X = to take up X).
segments.s031-g48: عنان باره تیزتک را سپرد is a slightly compressed older order meaning 'he handed the reins to Tiztak.' عنان ('reins') is the object being handed; باره here is a noun meaning 'load/burden' or can function as a qualifying word in older usage (in context it accompanies عنان as part of the transferred gear). تیزتک is a proper/name or epithet (here the recipient), and را is the object/definite marker used before the verb in classical word order; سپرد is the past of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over'. The whole phrase = 'he entrusted/gave the reins to Tiztak.'
segments.s031-g49: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person singular past ('you said'); گفتی here is a past-tense verb, not the noun sense listed. یکی + آتشستی is a noun phrase: یکی 'one / a certain (person/thing)' precedes the adjective آتشستی ('fiery, flame‑like'). درست is an emphatic adjective meaning 'true/proper' qualifying آتشستی. Word order is Subject (تو) + verb (گفتی) followed by the quoted description.
segments.s031-g50: که introduces a relative/clause-of-content ('that / who'). پیش نگهبان ایوان is a locative phrase 'in front of / before the guard of the iwan' (پیش = before/in front of, ایوان = porch/iwan). برست is a past (archaic/poetic) verb meaning 'burst / flared up / broke out' here; it completes the relative clause modifying 'one, a firelike thing'.
segments.s031-g51: گران گرز ('heavy mace') is an adjective + noun (گران modifies گرز). برداشت is the 3rd-person past 'he took up / lifted' (برداشت = lifting/removal sense). از پیش زین is a prepositional phrase 'from in front of the saddle' (از پیش = from before, زین = saddle). The whole segment is a simple past clause describing the action of taking up the mace.
segments.s031-g52: تو گفتی again is 'you said' (2nd-person past) reporting what was said. همی is an old/poetic particle that here gives an aspectual/intentional sense — it helps render the reported verb as prospective/continuous ('would / was going to'). بر + نوردد is a verbal construction: بر is a prepositional/coverb giving the sense 'over/against' and نوردد (present/future/optative form in classical style) means 'roll over / sweep across'; together بر نوردد زمین = 'sweep/roll over the earth/ground.' زمین is the object 'ground/earth.'
segments.s031-g53: کس here is used with negative sense 'no one' (contexted by نماند). از روزبانان 'of the heralds' is a partitive/genitive construction ('from the heralds'). به در means 'at/to the door/gate' (در = gate/door) and بر نماند is a compound verbal phrase: نماند is 3rd-person past 'did not remain / was not left' with the particle بر as an intensifier/prefixed element; the clause means 'no one of the heralds remained at the gate.'
segments.s031-g54: فریدون is the personal name (subject/theme). جهان‌آفرین is an epithet/appositive 'Creator of the World' attached to the name. را is the definite object/topic marker placed after the epithet here (typical epic word order for a called/announced person). بخواند is the verb in past narrative form meaning 'called / summoned' (third-person past); thus the clause means 'they called Fereydun, the Creator of the World.'
segments.s031-g55: به اسب اندر آمد is a fronted locative + verb: به اسب 'on horseback' (به = on/with) + اندر آمد 'entered / came in' (past). The second به + کاخ بزرگ is the directional/locative 'into the great palace' (به = into, کاخ بزرگ = great palace). The word order places the manner ('on horseback') before the motion verb and the destination after it, a typical poetic inversion.
segments.s031-g56: This is a compact descriptive phrase: جهان ناسپرده is an adjectival phrase meaning 'not revealed/known to the world' (ناسپرده = unentrusted/unrevealed to the world), modifying جوان سترگ ('young' + سترگ 'large/striking eye' or 'mighty-sighted'). Together they form an epithet: 'a young, great‑eyed man unknown to the world.' Word order is typical epic apposition: world‑qualifier + noun + adjectives.
segments.s031-g33: This phrase begins with the preposition ز + a measurement phrase (ز یک میل = 'from one mile away'), followed by the light verb کرد and the verbal noun نگاه. In classical/poetic Persian the light verb کرد 'made/did' combines with نگاه 'a look/gaze' to mean 'cast a look'; the subject آفریدون appears after the verb because of inverted/poetic word order.
segments.s031-g34: یکی is an indefinite determiner here ('a certain'), followed by کاخ 'palace' as the direct object and the past verb دید 'saw' (3rd sg. past). اندر آن شهر is a prepositional phrase 'in that city'; the following شاه functions as the possessor ('the king'), so the whole phrase reads 'a certain palace in that city — the king's'.
segments.s031-g35: فروزنده is an adjective/epithet 'radiant' placed before a simile introduced by چون ('like'), مشتری here = the planet Jupiter. بر سپهر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the sky/heavens.' The segment is a compact simile: 'radiant like Jupiter on the heavens.'
segments.s031-g36: همه جای is an adverbial expression 'everywhere' (literally 'all place'), followed by a coordinated list of abstract nouns: شادی 'joy', آرام 'calm/peace', مهر 'love/affection'. In Persian poetry the copula is often omitted, so the nominal string functions descriptively ('everywhere [was] joy and calm and love').
segments.s031-g37: که here is a relative/complementizer ('that/whose') linking this clause to the preceding context. ایوانش is ایوان + enclitic ـش 'his/its' (the porch belonging to him). برتر ز کیوان is a comparative phrase: برتر 'higher/superior' + ز (poetic 'than') + کیوان (the planet Saturn). نمود is the classical past verb 'نمود' (= 'نمودن' in past) meaning 'appeared/was shown' and serves as the clause's predicate, so the structure reads 'that his porch seemed higher than Kiwan.'
segments.s031-g38: که introduces a reported clause ('that'); گفتی here is classical/poetic and functions as a reportive/quotative marker ('it is/was said' or 'one said'), not the modern noun sense; ستاره is the grammatical subject 'the star'; بخواهد is the present/future-subjunctive form of خواستن used here to mean 'will/wishes to' or 'will incline to'; بسود is a postpositional/adverbial phrase 'to/for advantage'. Word order follows classical Persian: reportive verb + subject + verbal predicate.
segments.s031-g39: بدانست is the past tense verb 'he knew' / 'he perceived' serving as the main verb. کان is an archaic particle introducing the following substantive clause (classical use similar to 'that' or 'that indeed'). خانه اژدهاست is a nominal clause: خانه 'house' + اژدهاست (اژدها + است fused) 'is (of) a dragon' — i.e. 'the house belonged to/was a dragon's.'
segments.s031-g40: که again introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('that/which'); جای بزرگی is a genitive-like nominal phrase meaning 'a place of greatness' (جای 'place' + بزرگی 'greatness'); و 'and' links the coordinated noun phrase; جای بهاست is 'a place of value' where بها + است is fused (بهاست) meaning 'is valuable/has worth.' The structure describes qualities of the house just mentioned.
segments.s031-g17: هم آنگه (an emphatic temporal phrase) = 'even then' / 'just then'. میان کیانی is a prepositional phrase 'in the midst of the Kiyāns' (میان = 'among/in the middle of', کیانی = 'the Kayanian/royal [troops]'). ببست is a past tense third-person singular of بستن used here to mean 'he closed/pressed together' or 'he drew himself into/among' — the verb comes at the end of the clause, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s031-g18: بران باره = 'on that occasion' (بران = 'upon that', باره = 'occasion'). تیزتک here names the swift little mount (a compound adjective/noun), followed by the preposition/coverb بر + نشست (نشست = past 'sat/mounted'); the phrase تیزتک بر نشست thus means 'he sat upon / mounted (the) Tiztak'. This uses the regular past tense verb نشست with بر to indicate mounting.
segments.s031-g19: سرش تیز شد is an idiomatic past construction: سرش = 'his head' where سر = 'inner thought/mind' (here metaphorical), ش = possessive 'his'; تیز شد = 'became sharp/keen' (شد = past of 'to become'). کینه و جنگ را follows as the motive/content ('hatred and battle' marked by the object marker را) — the object is put after the predicate, a common poetic/order-flexible pattern.
segments.s031-g20: به آب اندر افگند گلرنگ را is a fairly literal sequence: به آب اندر = 'into the water' (به + اندر, where اندر is the archaic/informal 'in/within'), افگند = past 'threw / cast', and گلرنگ را is the definite object (marked with را). Word order places the directional phrase before the verb and the object after it.
segments.s031-g21: ببستند is the past plural of بستن ('they bound/tied'); یارانش = 'his companions' (یاران + possessive ش), یکسر = 'all at once/entirely', and کمر = 'waist/loin/girdle' — together they mean 'his companions completely girded their loins.' The verb precedes or follows the object depending on emphasis; here the past plural verb subito indicates collective action.
segments.s031-g22: همیدون is a proper name (the person or thing placed); به دریا = 'toward/to the sea' (به + دریا), نهادند is past plural 'they placed / set' (نهادن), and سر here is 'head' (literal or idiomatic as in 'set head [toward something]'). Grammatically نهادند is the finite verb and همیدون and سر are its complements, producing 'they set Homaydūn's head toward the sea' (i.e. plunged or thrust toward the sea).
segments.s031-g23: بر آن بادپایان = 'upon those fleet-footed ones' (بر آن = 'on/upon those', بادپایان = 'the swift-footed' — an epithet for horses or runners). با آفرین = 'with acclamation/bravo' (با = 'with', آفرین used as a shout or exclamation). The phrase sets the scene/manner (upon the swift mounts, with shouts).
segments.s031-g24: به آب اندرون = 'into the water' (به + اندرون 'inside/within'), غرقه کردند is a causative/perfective construction meaning 'they made (it) sink / they drowned / they plunged', and زین = 'saddle' (direct object). Note that there is no را here — classical/poetic Persian often omits the object-marker — so the object appears at the clause end after the verb.

sentences.s031-s1: When he came near the Arvand River, he sent greetings to the boatmen.
sentences.s031-s2: To that the boatman said, 'O victorious king, the ship, having been launched, is already on its way.'
sentences.s031-s3: Bring me there with my army; do not prevent any of these from coming to this side.
sentences.s031-s4: Bring me there so that I may find a crossing over the water, by ship and by boat as well, in haste.
sentences.s031-s13: They reached dry land; the leader(s) of the grudge‑seekers set their face toward Jerusalem.
sentences.s031-s14: For they hurled words at the champion (they insulted the hero), and indeed they called him 'Kang Džeḥodjaš' (an obscure/corrupted name).
sentences.s031-s15: They now, in Arabic, called it the 'Pure House,' and they considered it Zahhāk's raised iwan.
sentences.s031-s16: When they came from the plain near the city, from that city a seeker came forth toward them.
sentences.s031-s5: The river's guardian did not bring a boat and did not come; Fereydun cried, "Alight!"
sentences.s031-s6: He answered thus: that the king of the world had spoken to me in private.
sentences.s031-s7: ‘Do not let a single gnat in until — first — you obtain permission and fix a proper mahr (marriage portion).’
sentences.s031-s8: When Fereydun heard (this) he became enraged; of that deep sea no fear came to him (he was not afraid).
sentences.s031-s21: He told his companions that whoever heaped dark earth would thus raise a high place out of the abyss.
sentences.s031-s22: I am indeed afraid, for perhaps the world harbors a secret with him, one hidden within.
sentences.s031-s23: We must hurry into this narrow place; a day's delay would (befall us).
sentences.s031-s24: He spoke, seized the heavy mace, and handed the reins to Tiztak.
sentences.s031-s25: You said there was a single true fire that flared up before the guard of the iwan.
sentences.s031-s26: He lifted a heavy mace from before the saddle; you said he would sweep it over the ground.
sentences.s031-s27: No one of the heralds remained at the gate; they called Fereydun, the Creator of the World.
sentences.s031-s28: He came into the great palace on horseback — a mighty young man unknown to the world.
sentences.s031-s17: From a mile away Afrīdūn cast his gaze; he beheld a certain palace in that city — the king's.
sentences.s031-s18: Radiant like Jupiter upon the heavens, everywhere there was joy and calm and love.
sentences.s031-s19: whose porch appeared higher than Kiwan, for (it was) said that the star would incline to advantage.
sentences.s031-s20: He knew that that house was of a dragon, that it was a place of greatness and a place of value.
sentences.s031-s9: Then, in the midst of the Kayanian ranks he closed in; on that occasion he mounted the swift little steed.
sentences.s031-s10: His mind grew keen for revenge and battle, and he hurled Golrang into the water.
sentences.s031-s11: His companions at once girded their loins; they set Homaydūn's head toward the sea.
sentences.s031-s12: Upon those fleet-footed (horses), with cries of 'bravo', they plunged the saddles into the water.
